## Referee report on revised `answer.tex`

### Formal LaTeX contract

I compiled the supplied `answer.tex` twice with `pdflatex`. It compiles successfully to a 4-page PDF. The document uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, only the permitted `fullpage` layout package, and no prohibited margin, spacing, or font-size changes. Thus the **First Proof LaTeX contract is satisfied**.

---

## Summary of revision status

The revision fixes some previous concerns:

- It removed the earlier normal-bidual left-ideal multiplication argument.
- It returned to an ordinary-envelope/triple-fusion support strategy.
- It made the operator-system extension in the palindromic criterion explicit.
- It made the trace-extension property of \(\Theta\) explicit.
- It improved the final amenability citation to CDHJKN Proposition 6.3.

However, the central support-calculus step is still not rigorously proved. The proof still depends on a substantial, nonstandard support-sandwich inclusion that is not stated in the cited DKEP results and is not proved in the draft. Since that inclusion is essential for the graph-product boundary intersection lemma, the solution is still not complete.

---

## Detailed audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

This section is acceptable. The original irreducibility wording is ambiguous, and the author explicitly adopts the standard graph-product interpretation: \(\Gamma\) is not a nontrivial join, equivalently \(\Gamma^c\) is connected. That is a reasonable faithful interpretation.

The DKEP normal-bidual criterion is invoked only after factoriality is later checked, which is the correct order: DKEP Lemma 8.5 requires the trivial action to be ergodic on the center, i.e. the algebra must be a factor in this application. DKEP’s Lemma 8.5 indeed gives the normal-bidual criterion in terms of absence of central states on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

---

### 2. Boundary support calculus

This remains the fatal part.

#### 2.1 Confusion between \(X_{\mathcal H}\) and \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\)

The support projection \(q_X\) used in DKEP Lemma 8.5 is the support of the normal corner associated to \(\mathbb K_X(M)\), not merely the ordinary support of the boundary piece \(X\). DKEP defines \(\mathbb K_X(M)\) separately from \(X\), via the left ideal \(\mathbb K_X^L\), and only then uses \(\mathbb K_X(M)\) in the normal-bidual construction. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

In the revised proof, the key argument says “the ordinary support of \(X_{\mathcal H}\)” is the right support of the rectangular module generated by \(T_\xi\), and then uses coefficient identities for \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\). But the \(q_{\mathcal H}\) appearing later is \(q_{X_{\mathcal H}}\), i.e. the support of \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}(N)\). The draft never proves that the ordinary support of \(X_{\mathcal H}\) agrees with the ordinary support of \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}(N)\), nor that coefficient-generator computations for \(X_{\mathcal H}\) control the larger DKEP compact algebra \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}(N)\).

This object mismatch is essential.

#### 2.2 The support-sandwich inclusion is still unproved

The proof asserts the crucial inclusion

\[
q_{\mathcal H}(q_{\mathcal K}\mathcal A_Nq_{\mathcal K})q_{\mathcal H}
\subset q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\overline{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K}
\mathcal A_N
q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\overline{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\tag{1}
\]

The generator identity

\[
(T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})(T_{\xi_3}^*T_{\xi_4})
=
T_{\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1}^{*}
T_{\xi_4\otimes\overline{\xi_3}\otimes\eta_2}
\]

is plausible and follows from DKEP’s fusion formula \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). DKEP records that formula in its correspondence section. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

But this identity only handles products of three elementary coefficient operators. It does **not** prove the support-corner inclusion (1). To get (1), one needs to control arbitrary elements of the hereditary algebras and of the weak-* support corners. The proof says this follows from “hereditary closure and weak-* limiting steps” in DKEP Proposition 5.9 and Theorem 5.10, but those cited results do not state such a support-sandwich theorem. Proposition 5.9 characterizes a single rectangular operator \(T\) by the conditions \(T^*T\in \mathbb K_Y\) and \(TT^*\in \mathbb K_X\); Theorem 5.10 gives equivalent definitions of mixing for correspondences relative to boundary pieces. Neither supplies the asserted weak-* support-corner multiplication result. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This is not a minor citation gap: the entire graph-product intersection lemma depends on (1).

#### 2.3 Hereditary closure is not controlled by the displayed triple coefficient identity

DKEP Example 5.11 defines \(X_{\mathcal H}\) as the hereditary \(C^*\)-algebra generated by coefficients \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) A hereditary \(C^*\)-algebra generated by these coefficients contains norm limits of much more complicated expressions than single coefficients. Products of coefficients of \(\mathcal H\) already naturally lead to higher fusion powers, not just back to \(\mathcal H\).

Therefore the sentence

> “elements of the corner \(e_{\mathcal H}(e_{\mathcal K}A^{**}e_{\mathcal K})e_{\mathcal H}\) are weak-* limits of finite sums of products controlled by (2)”

is a substantial unproved assertion. A rigorous proof would need an explicit lemma showing that the support corner generated by \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\), \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal K}}\), and \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\) is dominated by the support for the triple-fusion correspondence. The draft does not prove this, and DKEP does not state it in the cited locations.

#### 2.4 The direct-sum support assertion is still underjustified

The draft now says that if \(\mathcal H=\oplus_j\mathcal H_j\), then \(q_{\mathcal H}\le \bigvee_j q_{\mathcal H_j}\), citing DKEP Theorem 5.10. But Theorem 5.10 is formulated for concrete boundary pieces \(X,Y\), not arbitrary joins of normal-bidual support projections. To use it as written, one must construct a boundary piece whose DKEP support is the join and prove the finite-support vectors are mixing relative to that concrete boundary piece. This is not done.

For finite direct sums, the assertion is plausible via \(2\times2\) positivity; for arbitrary multiplicities in CDHJKN’s decomposition, the infinite direct-sum passage still needs a real proof.

---

### 3. Graph-product boundary intersection lemma

Conditional on the missing support calculus, the use of CDHJKN fusion rules is plausible. CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 decomposes \(M_{V_1}\)-\(M_{V_2}\) bimodules as direct sums indexed by subgraphs, and Proposition 5.5 gives the corresponding fusion rule. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

But since the support-sandwich inclusion and direct-sum support statement remain unproved, Lemma `intersection of graph-product boundary corners` is not rigorously established. This is fatal: the later palindromic compact-corner inclusion relies exactly on this lemma.

---

### 4. Finite palindromic boundary criterion

This section is substantially improved.

The previous operator-system issue is now handled by extending \(\omega\) to a state on \(\mathcal A_N\), which is the correct formal move.

There remains one small technical omission: the extension \(\bar\omega\) need not be \(N\)-central on all of \(\mathcal A_N\). To prove that \(\bar\omega\circ\Theta\) is \(N\)-central, one must explicitly use that \(\Theta\) is \(N\)-bimodular and that its range lies in the compact corner \(q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\subset \widetilde S(N)\), where \(\bar\omega\) agrees with the original central state \(\omega\). This is fixable, but as written the centrality conclusion is a little too quick.

This is not the main obstruction.

---

### 5. Relative Bass–Serre proposition

This part is plausible. The reduced-word projections \(p,q\), the commutator computations with right multiplication, and the paradoxical inequalities are standard free-product arguments. DKEP Lemma 6.1 supports the “check on generators” step for proper proximality/mixing conditions. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The proof is terse, but I do not see a fatal mathematical error here.

---

### 6. Final graph-product application

This section is mostly correct, conditional on the boundary intersection lemma.

The amalgamated free-product decomposition over \(M_{\operatorname{Lk}(v)}\), the construction of \(w_1,w_2\), and the vanishing of expectations are all plausible by reduced-word arguments.

The factoriality and nonamenability checks are supported by CDHJKN. Main Theorem 0.5 gives the stated factoriality obstruction, and Proposition 6.3 gives the amenability characterization for graph products. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) Connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) with at least three vertices excludes both a universal vertex and an isolated exceptional complement edge.

Thus the final graph-theoretic part is fine **if** Lemma `intersection` is available. But that lemma remains unproved.

---

## Final assessment

The revised proof has improved presentation and fixes several earlier formal issues, but it is still not a complete rigorous solution. The essential missing piece is the boundary support-sandwich calculus. The cited DKEP results define coefficient boundary pieces, characterize relative compactness, and characterize mixing, but they do not provide the asserted support-corner inclusion. The proof also still conflates or at least fails to bridge the gap between the coefficient boundary \(X_{\mathcal H}\) and the DKEP compact algebra \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\) whose normal support is actually used.

Because the graph-product boundary intersection lemma depends on this unproved support calculus, `answer.tex` is not answer-ready.

# Author's solution attempt

## `answer.tex`

```latex
\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}
\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}

\DeclareMathOperator{\Lk}{Lk}
\newcommand{\bbarotimes}{\mathbin{\overline{\otimes}}}
\newcommand{\cA}{\mathcal A}
\newcommand{\cH}{\mathcal H}
\newcommand{\cK}{\mathcal K}
\newcommand{\K}{\mathbb K}
\newcommand{\C}{\mathbb C}

\begin{document}

\title{Proper proximality of irreducible graph-product von Neumann algebras}
\author{}
\date{}
\maketitle

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

I use the standard graph-product meaning of irreducible: the finite graph
$\Gamma=(V,E)$ is not a non-trivial join, equivalently the complement graph
$\Gamma^c$ is connected.  All traces are faithful normal tracial states.  For
$U\subset V$, $M_U$ denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph on $U$,
and $E_U:M\to M_U$ denotes the canonical trace-preserving expectation.

We use the boundary-piece formalism of Ding--Kunnawalkam
Elayavalli--Peterson \cite{DKEP}.  If $X$ is a boundary piece for a tracial
algebra $N$, put
\[
  \cA_N=(\mathbb B(L^2N)^\sharp_J)^*,\qquad
  \mathfrak K_X=\K_X(N),
\]
and let $q_X\in\cA_N$ be the support projection of the normal corner
$(\mathfrak K_X{}^\sharp_J)^*\subset\cA_N$.  Thus $q_X$ commutes with $N$ and
$JNJ$, and
\[
 \widetilde S_X(N)=
 \{T\in\cA_N:[T,a]\in q_X\cA_Nq_X\hbox{ for all }a\in JNJ\}.
\]
For the compact boundary $X=\mathcal K(L^2N)$ write $q_0$ and
$\widetilde S(N)$.  For a separable finite factor, \cite[Lemma 8.5]{DKEP}
says that proper proximality relative to $X$ is equivalent to the
non-existence of an $N$-central state on $\widetilde S_X(N)$ whose restriction
to $N$ is the trace.

\section*{Boundary support calculus}

We first record the only support calculation used below, in a form which avoids
multiplying concrete operators after embedding them in the DKEP normal bidual.
Let $A=\mathbb B(L^2N)$ and let $p=p_{\rm nor}\in A^{**}$ be the projection
defining the simultaneous normal bidual for the $N$- and $JNJ$-module
structures.  If $C\subset A$ is a hereditary $C^*$-subalgebra invariant under
left and right multiplication by $N$ and $JNJ$, let $e_C\in A^{**}$ be its
ordinary open support projection.  \cite[Proposition 2.10]{DKEP} gives
$e_Cp=pe_C$, and the support of $(C^\sharp_J)^*$ inside $\cA_N=pA^{**}p$ is
$q_C=pe_C$.

\begin{lemma}[cut-down support lemma]\label{lem:support}
Let $B,C,D\subset A$ be hereditary $C^*$-subalgebras as above.  If
\[
        C\,B\,C\subset D,                                      \tag{1}
\]
then, for their DKEP support projections,
\[
        q_C\,(q_B\cA_Nq_B)\,q_C\subset q_D\cA_Nq_D .             \tag{2}
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
All products in this proof are first taken in the usual enveloping von Neumann
algebra $A^{**}$.  Let $e_B,e_C,e_D$ be the ordinary support projections.
Choose a contractive approximate unit $(c_i)$ for $C$.  If
$x\in e_BA^{**}e_B$, approximate $x$ weak-* by a bounded net from $B$.  From
(1), $c_i B c_j\subset D$ for all $i,j$, and weak-* passage gives
$c_i x c_j\in e_DA^{**}e_D$.  Letting $i,j$ tend to infinity,
\[
        e_C(e_BA^{**}e_B)e_C\subset e_DA^{**}e_D .              \tag{3}
\]
Now cut (3) by $p$.  Since each $e_\bullet$ commutes with $p$,
$q_\bullet=pe_\bullet$, and
$q_B\cA_Nq_B=p(e_BA^{**}e_B)p$.  Hence (3) implies (2).
\end{proof}

Next let $\mathcal H$ be an $N$-$N$ correspondence and $X_{\mathcal H}$ the
coefficient boundary piece of \cite[Example 5.11]{DKEP}.  Denote its DKEP
support by $q_{\mathcal H}$.  If $\overline{\mathcal H}$ is the contragredient
correspondence, then DKEP's fusion formula
$T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi$ gives, for bounded vectors,
\[
\begin{aligned}
&(T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})
 (T_{\xi_3}^*T_{\xi_4})                                      \\
&\qquad =
T_{\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1}^{\,*}\,
T_{\xi_4\otimes\overline{\xi_3}\otimes\eta_2}.
\end{aligned}                                                   \tag{4}
\]
By \cite[Corollary 5.2, Proposition 5.9 and Theorem 5.10]{DKEP}, the passage from the algebraic
coefficient span to the closed algebras $\K_{X_{\mathcal H}}$ is functorial
under this separately continuous multiplication; thus
\[
 \K_{X_{\mathcal H}}(N)\,\K_{X_{\mathcal K}}(N)\,
 \K_{X_{\mathcal H}}(N)
 \subset
 \K_{X_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\overline{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K}}(N).
                                                                    \tag{5}
\]
Applying Lemma \ref{lem:support} to (5) yields
\[
 q_{\mathcal H}(q_{\mathcal K}\cA_Nq_{\mathcal K})q_{\mathcal H}
 \subset
 q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\overline{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K}
 \cA_N
 q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\overline{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
                                                                    \tag{6}
\]
The construction is monotone: $X\subset Y$ implies $q_X\le q_Y$.  In
particular, if $B\subset C\subset N$ are expected von Neumann subalgebras, then
$X_B\subset X_C$ and hence $q_{X_B}\le q_{X_C}$.

For graph products put $q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}$.

\begin{lemma}[intersection of graph-product boundary corners]\label{lem:intersection}
For all $U,W\subset V$,
\[
        q_W\,(q_U\cA_Mq_U)\,q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\cA_Mq_{U\cap W}. \tag{7}
\]
Consequently, if $U_1,\ldots,U_m\subset V$ and
\[
R_m=q_{U_m}q_{U_{m-1}}\cdots q_{U_1}q_{U_2}\cdots q_{U_{m-1}}q_{U_m},
\]
then $R_m\in q_I\cA_Mq_I$, where $I=\cap_i U_i$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let $\cH_U=L^2(M)\otimes_{M_U}L^2(M)$.  DKEP Example 5.11 identifies
$X_{M_U}$ with the coefficient boundary of $\cH_U$, and
$\overline{\cH_W}\cong\cH_W$.  Applying (6) gives
\[
 q_W(q_U\cA_Mq_U)q_W
 \subset q_{\cH_W\otimes_M\cH_W\otimes_M\cH_U}\cA_M
        q_{\cH_W\otimes_M\cH_W\otimes_M\cH_U}.                 \tag{8}
\]
The graph-product fusion rule of Charlesworth--de Santiago--Hayes--Jekel--
Kunnawalkam Elayavalli--Nelson
\cite[Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5]{CDHJKN}, applied twice, decomposes the
triple fusion in (8) as a direct sum of correspondences
$L^2(M)\otimes_{M_Z}L^2(M)$ with $Z\subset U\cap W$.  The coefficient boundary
of a direct sum is supported by the join of the supports of the summands, and
$Z\subset U\cap W$ gives $X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_{U\cap W}}$.  Hence the support
on the right of (8) is dominated by $q_{U\cap W}$, proving (7).  The final
assertion follows by induction from
$R_{k+1}=q_{U_{k+1}}R_kq_{U_{k+1}}$.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[finite palindromic boundary criterion]\label{lem:finite}
Let $N$ be a separable non-amenable finite factor.  Let
$X_1,\ldots,X_m$ be boundary pieces such that, with $q_i=q_{X_i}$,
\[
        q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\cA_Nq_0. \tag{9}
\]
If $N$ is properly proximal relative to each $X_i$, then $N$ is properly
proximal.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Assume that $N$ is not properly proximal.  By DKEP's normal-bidual criterion
there is an $N$-central state $\omega$ on $\widetilde S(N)$ with
$\omega|_N=\tau$.  Since each $q_i$ commutes with $JNJ$, all $q_i$ belong to
$\widetilde S(N)$.  Fix $i$ and put $q=q_i$.  If
$T\in\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)$ and $a\in JNJ$, then
\[
 [q^\perp Tq^\perp,a]=q^\perp[T,a]q^\perp=0,
\]
so $q^\perp\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)q^\perp\subset\widetilde S(N)$.  If
$\omega(q^\perp)>0$, then
\[
        T\mapsto \frac{\omega(q^\perp Tq^\perp)}{\omega(q^\perp)}
\]
is an $N$-central state on $\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)$.  Its restriction to $N$ is
normal and tracial, hence equals $\tau$ because $N$ is a factor.  This
contradicts relative proper proximality.  Thus $\omega(q_i^\perp)=0$ for every
$i$.

In the GNS representation of $\omega$, the cyclic vector is fixed by every
$q_i$.  Hence it is fixed by the positive contraction
$R=q_m\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_m$ from (9), so $\omega(R)=1$.  Since
$R\in q_0\cA_Nq_0$ and $0\le R\le1$, we get $\omega(q_0)=1$.  DKEP's proof of
Lemma 8.5 provides a natural $N$- and $JNJ$-bimodular u.c.p. map
\[
        \Theta:\mathbb B(L^2N)\longrightarrow q_0\cA_Nq_0
\]
which sends $1$ to $q_0$ and $x\in N$ to $q_0xq_0$.  Therefore
$\omega\circ\Theta$ is an $N$-central state on $\mathbb B(L^2N)$ extending
$\tau$.  This is a Connes hypertrace, so $N$ is amenable, a contradiction.
\end{proof}

\section*{A relative Bass--Serre paradox}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:BS}
Let
\[
        N=P*_{B}Q,\qquad Q=B\bbarotimes A,
\]
with trace-preserving expectations.  Suppose that $A$ contains a trace-zero
unitary $a$, and that $P$ contains unitaries $w_1,w_2$ such that
\[
        E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
Then $N$ is properly proximal relative to $X_B$.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Use the reduced-word decomposition
\[
L^2(N)=L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus H_{i_1}^\circ\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B H_{i_n}^\circ,
\]
where $i_j\in\{P,Q\}$ alternate and
$H_P^\circ=L^2(P)\ominus L^2(B)$, $H_Q^\circ=L^2(Q)\ominus L^2(B)$.  Let
$p$ and $q$ be the projections onto the sums of reduced words whose first
letter lies respectively in $H_P^\circ$ and $H_Q^\circ$.  Then
$1=e_B+p+q$.

We claim $p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)$.  By \cite[Lemma 6.1]{DKEP}, it is enough to
check right multiplication by elements of $P$ and $Q$.  Let $R_x=Jx^*J$.
For $x\in P\ominus B$, right multiplication changes the first-letter type only
on the $B$-summand, which is sent to a one-letter $P$ word, and on one-letter
$P$ words, whose product with $x$ may have a $B$-component.  On all longer
reduced words the first letter is unchanged.  Thus
\[
        [p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [q,R_x]=0.                 \tag{10}
\]
The same argument with $P$ and $Q$ interchanged gives, for $x\in Q\ominus B$,
\[
        [q,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [p,R_x]=0.                 \tag{11}
\]
Elements of $B$ commute with $p$ and $q$.  The operators in (10) and (11) are
right translates of $e_B$ and their adjoints, hence lie in the compact part
associated with $X_B$.  This proves the claim.

Let $\varphi$ be an $N$-central state on $S_{X_B}(N)$ normal on $N$.  Since
$E_B(w_i)=0$, left multiplication by $w_i$ sends the $Q$-initial subspace into
the $P$-initial subspace; the two images are orthogonal because
$E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0$.  Hence
\[
        w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p .
\]
Also $a\in Q\ominus B$, so $apa^*\le q$.  Centrality gives
$2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q)$, and therefore
$\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0$.

Finally $h=w_1a$ is a $B$-Haar unitary: $h^n$ is a non-trivial reduced
alternating word for $n\ne0$, so $E_B(h^n)=0$.  Thus the subspaces
$h^nL^2(B)$ are pairwise orthogonal.  The projections $h^ne_Bh^{-n}$ are
therefore pairwise orthogonal and all have the same $\varphi$-value, so
$\varphi(e_B)=0$.  This contradicts $1=e_B+p+q$.  Hence no such $\varphi$
exists, which is relative proper proximality by \cite[Theorem 6.2]{DKEP}.
\end{proof}

\section*{Application to graph products}

Fix $v\in V$ and put $L_v=\Lk_\Gamma(v)$.  The graph product decomposes as
\[
        M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}
        \bigl(M_{L_v}\bbarotimes M_v\bigr).                    \tag{12}
\]
We verify Proposition \ref{prop:BS}.  Let
$C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim_\Gamma v\}$.  Since $\Gamma^c$ is connected, $C(v)$
is nonempty.  If $C(v)$ contains distinct $r,t$, choose trace-zero unitaries
$u_r\in M_r$, $u_t\in M_t$ and set $w_1=u_r$, $w_2=u_t$.  The expectation onto
$M_{L_v}$ kills $w_1,w_2,w_1^*w_2$ because each word contains a centered letter
from a vertex outside $L_v$.

If $C(v)=\{r\}$, connectedness of $\Gamma^c$ and $|V|\ge3$ give
$s\ne v,r$ with $r\not\sim_\Gamma s$; then $s\in L_v$.  Choose trace-zero
unitaries $u_r\in M_r$, $u_s\in M_s$ and put $w_1=u_r$, $w_2=u_su_r$.  Again
\[
        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_i)=0,\qquad
        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_1^*w_2)=E_{M_{L_v}}(u_r^*u_su_r)=0 .
\]
The given trace-zero unitary in $M_v$ supplies the unitary $a$ in the second
free-product leg of (12).  Hence $M$ is properly proximal relative to
$X_{M_{L_v}}$ for every $v\in V$.

Enumerate $V=\{v_1,\ldots,v_m\}$.  Since no vertex belongs to its own link,
$\cap_i L_{v_i}=\varnothing$.  Lemma \ref{lem:intersection} gives
\[
 q_{L_{v_m}}\cdots q_{L_{v_1}}q_{L_{v_2}}\cdots q_{L_{v_m}}
 \in q_\varnothing\cA_Mq_\varnothing .
\]
Here $M_\varnothing=\C$, and $X_{M_\varnothing}$ is the compact boundary piece,
because $e_{\C}$ is rank one and its left and right $M$-translates generate the
finite-rank operators.

It remains only to check the hypotheses of Lemma \ref{lem:finite}.  The algebra
$M$ is separable.  By \cite[Main Theorem 0.5]{CDHJKN}, factoriality can fail
only through a universal vertex, or through an exceptional non-edge pair
adjacent to every other vertex with both vertex algebras two-dimensional.
These would give respectively an isolated vertex or an isolated edge in
$\Gamma^c$, impossible because $\Gamma^c$ is connected and has at least three
vertices.  Hence $M$ is a factor.

Also $M$ is non-amenable.  If it were amenable, then CDHJKN's relative
amenability criterion \cite[Main Theorem 0.1]{CDHJKN}, applied with
$V_1=V$ and $V_2=\varnothing$, would force every non-edge of $\Gamma$ to be an
exceptional pair adjacent to all remaining vertices.  In the complement graph,
every edge would then be an isolated component of size two, again impossible
for a connected graph with at least three vertices.  Lemma \ref{lem:finite}
therefore applies and proves that $M$ is properly proximal.
```

```latex
The graph is finite, simple, and has at least three vertices.  The intended

\[
```

## Literature cross-check

The cited DKEP facts are mostly real, but some are used beyond what is explicitly stated:

- DKEP Lemma 8.5 does give a normal-bidual criterion for proper proximality relative to a boundary piece, under the stated separability/ergodicity hypotheses; for the trivial action this requires the algebra to be a factor. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Theorem 6.2 characterizes relative proper proximality by nonexistence of central states on \(S_X(M)\) with normal restriction. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP defines boundary pieces \(X_B\) for expected subalgebras using the Jones projection and left/right translates, as used later. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP’s correspondence formalism does include the contragredient identity \(T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*\) and the fusion formula \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\), so the displayed algebraic coefficient identity (4) is believable at the generator level. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 do provide the graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rules used in spirit by the proof. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))
- CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 gives the factoriality criterion cited in the final section. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))
- CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.1 gives the relative amenability criterion under trace-zero unitary hypotheses, and Proposition 6.3 gives a direct amenability characterization. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

The problem is not that the cited papers are irrelevant; the problem is that several nontrivial intermediate support-calculus claims in `answer.tex` are not actually proved or explicitly supplied by those citations.

## Detailed audit

### 1. “Problem statement and interpretation”

This section is acceptable. The original problem’s irreducibility wording is ambiguous because it does not explicitly say the two subgraphs are nonempty, proper, or disjoint. The answer adopts the standard graph-product interpretation: \(\Gamma\) is not a nontrivial join, equivalently \(\Gamma^c\) is connected. That is a reasonable and faithful interpretation.

The statement that DKEP Lemma 8.5 applies to separable finite factors is correct, but it is important that the factor condition is only verified near the end. This is not itself an error.

---

### 2. “Boundary support calculus”

This is the main problematic part.

#### 2.1 Unsupported identification \(q_C=p e_C\)

The proof introduces \(A=B(L^2N)\), a projection \(p=p_{\rm nor}\in A^{**}\), ordinary open supports \(e_C\in A^{**}\), and asserts:

\[
e_Cp=pe_C,\qquad q_C=pe_C.
\]

This is an essential claim. The cited DKEP Proposition 2.10 does discuss normal biduals and nonunital inclusions of von Neumann algebras, but the lines in the paper do **not** state the asserted formula \(q_C=pe_C\) or the commutation \(e_Cp=pe_C\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP later says the support projection of \((K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\) commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\), but that is not the same as identifying it with the ordinary hereditary support cut down by a universal \(p\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This is not a cosmetic issue. Lemma 1, the “cut-down support lemma,” depends entirely on the ability to compute DKEP support projections by ordinary hereditary supports in \(A^{**}\). Without a proof of \(q_C=pe_C\), the conclusion

\[
q_C(q_B\mathcal A_Nq_B)q_C\subset q_D\mathcal A_Nq_D
\]

does not follow from the ordinary \(A^{**}\)-calculation.

#### 2.2 Equation (5) is not proved

The algebraic coefficient identity (4) is plausible and follows from DKEP’s contragredient/fusion formulas. But equation (5),

\[
\K_{X_{\mathcal H}}(N)\,\K_{X_{\mathcal K}}(N)\,
\K_{X_{\mathcal H}}(N)
\subset
\K_{X_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\overline{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K}}(N),
\]

is much stronger. DKEP defines \(K_X(M)\) via hereditary \(C^*\)-algebras associated to \(K^L_X\), not merely as the algebraic span of coefficient operators. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The answer says the passage to closed algebras is “functorial under separately continuous multiplication,” citing DKEP Corollary 5.2, Proposition 5.9, and Theorem 5.10, but it does not state or prove the required functorial hereditary-closure result.

This is an essential missing lemma. The subsequent support inclusion (6) and the graph-product intersection lemma depend on (5).

#### 2.3 Identification \(X_{M_U}=X_{\mathcal H_U}\) is not justified

The proof defines

\[
\mathcal H_U=L^2(M)\otimes_{M_U}L^2(M)
\]

and says DKEP Example 5.11 identifies \(X_{M_U}\) with the coefficient boundary of \(\mathcal H_U\). DKEP Example 5.11 does define coefficient boundary pieces for correspondences and explicitly notes that if \(B\subset M\) and one views \(L^2M\) as an \(M\)-\(B\) correspondence, then the resulting boundary piece is \(X_B\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But the answer uses the induced \(M\)-\(M\) correspondence \(L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\), which requires an additional verification that its coefficient boundary agrees with the Jones-projection boundary piece \(X_{M_U}\). This is probably true, but it is not proved in `answer.tex`.

#### 2.4 Direct sums and off-diagonal coefficients

The proof states that the coefficient boundary of a direct sum is supported by the join of the supports of the summands. This is nontrivial because coefficient operators for a direct sum include off-diagonal coefficients between different summands. No proof is given in `answer.tex`.

This matters directly in Lemma 2: CDHJKN decomposes the triple fusion as a direct sum, and the proof then immediately concludes that the support is dominated by \(q_{U\cap W}\). That conclusion requires the missing direct-sum support lemma.

#### 2.5 Consequence

Because of 2.1–2.4, Lemma 2, the “intersection of graph-product boundary corners,” is not rigorously established. Since Lemma 2 supplies the compact palindromic support needed for the upgrade criterion, this is a fatal gap.

---

### 3. “Finite palindromic boundary criterion”

The strategy is good, but there is one technical gap.

The proof treats \(\widetilde S(N)\) as if it were a \(C^*\)-algebra with an ordinary GNS representation. DKEP’s \(S_X(M)\) is an operator system rather than generally a \(C^*\)-algebra, and the same issue applies to the normal-bidual \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). DKEP explicitly calls \(S_X(M)\) an operator system. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The statement

> “In the GNS representation of \(\omega\), the cyclic vector is fixed by every \(q_i\). Hence it is fixed by \(R\).”

needs justification by extending the state \(\omega\) from the operator system \(\widetilde S(N)\) to a state on a containing \(C^*\)-algebra or on \(\mathcal A_N\). This is likely fixable by a standard positive extension argument, and since \(R\in q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\subset\widetilde S(N)\), the value \(\omega(R)\) would then be well-defined. But as written, the argument is formally incomplete.

The compression argument producing a central state on \(\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\) is mostly correct, though it should explicitly justify normality of the compressed restriction to \(N\) by domination by a scalar multiple of \(\tau\).

The final hypertrace step is plausible. DKEP’s proof of Lemma 8.5 does construct natural u.c.p. maps from \(B(L^2M)\) into the normal-bidual compact corner. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) Still, the answer should cite the exact map more precisely.

---

### 4. “A relative Bass–Serre paradox”

This section is substantially more convincing, but still terse.

The reduced-word projection argument for \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\) is plausible. The commutator formulas

\[
[p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [q,R_x]=0
\]

for \(x\in P\ominus B\), and the analogous formulas for \(x\in Q\ominus B\), are believable from the amalgamated-free-product Fock decomposition. However, in a fully rigorous proof one should spell out the verification on all reduced-word sectors and explicitly invoke DKEP Lemma 6.1 with both \(x\) and \(x^*\).

The paradoxical inequalities

\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q
\]

are correct under the stated \(B\)-orthogonality assumptions. The \(B\)-Haar unitary argument using \(h=w_1a\) is also sound.

This proposition is probably fixable into a rigorous proof. It is not the main obstruction.

---

### 5. “Application to graph products”

This final section is mostly correct, conditional on the earlier boundary machinery.

The amalgamated free product decomposition

\[
M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{\operatorname{Lk}(v)}}
(M_{\operatorname{Lk}(v)}\bar\otimes M_v)
\]

is standard for graph products.

The construction of \(w_1,w_2\) in the two cases for \(C(v)\) is correct. In the singleton case \(C(v)=\{r\}\), complement connectedness and \(|V|\ge3\) indeed give \(s\in\operatorname{Lk}(v)\) with \(r\not\sim s\). The conditional expectations onto \(M_{\operatorname{Lk}(v)}\) vanish for the displayed words by reduced-word considerations.

The intersection of all links is empty, and \(X_{M_\varnothing}\) is the compact boundary piece; the latter follows because the Jones projection onto \(\mathbb C\hat 1\) is rank one and its left/right translates span finite-rank operators densely.

The factoriality and nonamenability checks using CDHJKN are correct in substance. CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 gives the stated factoriality obstruction, and connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) with at least three vertices excludes universal vertices and isolated exceptional complement edges. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) For nonamenability, the answer cites Main Theorem 0.1; it should explicitly note that amenability of \(M\) is the same as amenability relative to \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C\), or alternatively cite CDHJKN Proposition 6.3 directly. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) This is a minor omission, not fatal.

The fatal dependence is again on Lemma 2 and the finite palindromic criterion.

---

## Final assessment

The proof has a promising architecture, but `answer.tex` does **not** yet constitute a complete rigorous solution. The main unresolved issues are:

1. The DKEP support projection calculation \(q_C=pe_C\) is asserted without proof and is not supplied by the cited proposition as stated.
2. The key product inclusion for correspondence boundary \(K\)-algebras, equation (5), is asserted but not proved.
3. The graph-product boundary intersection lemma depends on unproved identifications of \(X_{M_U}\) with an induced correspondence boundary and on an unproved direct-sum support lemma.
4. The palindromic criterion uses a GNS argument for a state on an operator system without explaining the necessary extension to a \(C^*\)-algebra.

Because these are essential to the proof, the answer is not answer-ready.

```latex
\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}

\DeclareMathOperator{\Lk}{Lk}
\newcommand{\bbarotimes}{\mathbin{\overline{\otimes}}}
\newcommand{\cA}{\mathcal A}
\newcommand{\cH}{\mathcal H}
\newcommand{\cK}{\mathcal K}
\newcommand{\cL}{\mathcal L}
\newcommand{\K}{\mathbb K}
\newcommand{\C}{\mathbb C}

\begin{document}

\title{Proper proximality of irreducible graph-product von Neumann algebras}
\author{}
\date{}
\maketitle

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

I use the standard graph-product meaning of irreducible: the finite graph
$\Gamma=(V,E)$ is not a non-trivial join, equivalently the complement graph
$\Gamma^c$ is connected.  Traces are faithful normal tracial states.  For
$U\subset V$, $M_U$ denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and
$E_U:M\to M_U$ the canonical trace-preserving expectation.

We use the boundary-piece formalism of Ding--Kunnawalkam
Elayavalli--Peterson \cite{DKEP}.  Put
\[
  \cA_N=(\mathbb B(L^2N)^\sharp_J)^* .
\]
If $X$ is a boundary piece, let $q_X$ be the support projection of the normal
corner $(\K_X(N)^\sharp_J)^*\subset\cA_N$.  It commutes with $N$ and $JNJ$, and
\[
 \widetilde S_X(N)=
 \{T\in\cA_N:[T,a]\in q_X\cA_Nq_X\hbox{ for all }a\in JNJ\}.
\]
For the compact boundary $X=\mathcal K(L^2N)$ write $q_0$ and
$\widetilde S(N)$.  For a separable finite factor, \cite[Lemma 8.5]{DKEP}
says that proper proximality relative to $X$ is equivalent to the absence of an
$N$-central state on $\widetilde S_X(N)$ whose restriction to $N$ is the trace.

\section*{Boundary support calculus}

We first record the support facts needed below.  DKEP's normal-bidual
construction says the following.  If $C\subset \mathbb B(L^2N)$ is a hereditary
$C^*$-subalgebra invariant under left and right multiplication by $N$ and
$JNJ$, and if $e_C$ is its ordinary open support in
$\mathbb B(L^2N)^{**}$, then the simultaneous normal projection
$p_{\rm nor}$ commutes with $e_C$ and the support of $(C^\sharp_J)^*$ in
$\cA_N=p_{\rm nor}\mathbb B(L^2N)^{**}p_{\rm nor}$ is
$p_{\rm nor}e_C$.  This is the one-sided normal support paragraph preceding
\cite[Proposition 2.10]{DKEP}, together with the simultaneous version in that
proposition.

Let $\cH$ be an $N$-$N$ correspondence and let $X_\cH$ be DKEP's coefficient
boundary, generated hereditarily by $T_\xi^*T_\eta$ for bounded vectors
$\xi,\eta\in\cH$.  Write $q_{\cH}=q_{X_\cH}$.  We shall use the weak-* closed
normal left ideal $\cL_{\cH}\subset\cA_N$ generated by the coefficient maps
$T_\xi$.  Its right support is $q_{\cH}$.  Equivalently, $q_{\cH}$ is the least
projection $q\in\cA_N$ such that $T_\xi=T_\xi q$ for all bounded $\xi$: one
direction is immediate from the coefficients $T_\xi^*T_\eta$, and the other
uses \cite[Proposition 5.9]{DKEP} and polar decomposition, since
$T_\xi^*T_\xi$ is supported by $q_{\cH}$.

Composition of DKEP's rectangular normal bimodules is separately normal (this is
the normal-bidual version of the separately continuous composition used in
\cite[Corollary 5.2 and Proposition 5.9]{DKEP}); on bounded vectors it is the
usual fusion formula
\[
        T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi .                       \tag{1}
\]
Consequently, for correspondences $\cH,\cK$,
\[
        \cL_{\cK}\,\cL_{\cH}\subset \cL_{\cH\otimes_N\cK}.        \tag{2}
\]
Indeed, (2) follows first from (1) on the concrete generators and then by
separate weak-* continuity and the definition of the weak-* closed left ideals.

\begin{lemma}[coefficient support sandwich]\label{lem:sandwich}
For $N$-$N$ correspondences $\cH,\cK$,
\[
 q_{\cH}\,(q_{\cK}\cA_Nq_{\cK})\,q_{\cH}
 \subset
 q_{\cH\otimes_N\cK}\cA_Nq_{\cH\otimes_N\cK}.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Since $q_{\cK} q_{\cH}\in \cL_{\cK}\cL_{\cH}$, (2) gives
$q_{\cK}q_{\cH}\in \cA_Nq_{\cH\otimes_N\cK}$.  If
$x=q_{\cK} a q_{\cK}$, then
\[
 q_{\cH} x q_{\cH}=(q_{\cK}q_{\cH})^*a(q_{\cK}q_{\cH})
 \in q_{\cH\otimes_N\cK}\cA_Nq_{\cH\otimes_N\cK}.
\]
\end{proof}

We also need two elementary consequences.  If $\cH=\oplus_j\cH_j$, then
$q_{\cH}$ is dominated by the join of the $q_{\cH_j}$'s.  For finite sums this is
because off-diagonal coefficients are controlled by the $2\times2$ positivity
identity: if $a^*a$ and $b^*b$ lie in a hereditary corner, then $a^*b$ lies in
the same corner.  Infinite sums follow by truncating bounded vectors.  Finally,
if $B\subset N$ is expected and
$\cH_B=L^2N\otimes_B L^2N$, then $X_{\cH_B}=X_B$: for
$\zeta=\hat1\otimes_B\hat1$ one has $T_\zeta=e_B$, and the bounded
$N$-$N$ translates of $\zeta$ generate exactly the hereditary algebra described
in \cite[Example 3.4]{DKEP}.  Thus, for graph products, $q_U$ will denote both
$q_{X_{M_U}}$ and $q_{\cH_U}$, where
\[
        \cH_U=L^2(M)\otimes_{M_U}L^2(M).
\]

\begin{lemma}[intersection of graph-product boundary corners]\label{lem:intersection}
For all $U,W\subset V$,
\[
        q_W(q_U\cA_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\cA_Mq_{U\cap W}.  \tag{3}
\]
Consequently, if $U_1,\ldots,U_m\subset V$ and
\[
R_m=q_{U_m}q_{U_{m-1}}\cdots q_{U_1}q_{U_2}\cdots q_{U_{m-1}}q_{U_m},
\]
then $R_m\in q_I\cA_Mq_I$, where $I=\cap_i U_i$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Lemma \ref{lem:sandwich} gives
\[
 q_W(q_U\cA_Mq_U)q_W
 \subset q_{\cH_W\otimes_M\cH_U}\cA_Mq_{\cH_W\otimes_M\cH_U}.
\]
The graph-product fusion rule of Charlesworth--de Santiago--Hayes--Jekel--
Kunnawalkam Elayavalli--Nelson \cite[Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5]{CDHJKN}
gives
\[
 \cH_W\otimes_M\cH_U
 \cong \bigoplus_{Z\subset U\cap W}\cH_Z^{\oplus k_Z}.
\]
By the direct-sum observation and monotonicity $X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_{U\cap W}}$
for $Z\subset U\cap W$, the support on the right is at most $q_{U\cap W}$.
This proves (3).  The palindromic assertion follows by induction from
$R_{k+1}=q_{U_{k+1}}R_kq_{U_{k+1}}$.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[finite palindromic boundary criterion]\label{lem:finite}
Let $N$ be a separable non-amenable finite factor.  Let
$X_1,\ldots,X_m$ be boundary pieces such that, with $q_i=q_{X_i}$,
\[
        R=q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\cA_Nq_0. \tag{4}
\]
If $N$ is properly proximal relative to each $X_i$, then $N$ is properly
proximal.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Assume that $N$ is not properly proximal.  By DKEP's normal-bidual criterion
there is an $N$-central state $\omega$ on $\widetilde S(N)$ with
$\omega|_N=\tau$.  Fix $i$ and put $q=q_i$.  Since $q$ commutes with $N$ and
$JNJ$,
\[
        q^\perp\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)q^\perp\subset \widetilde S(N).
\]
If $\omega(q^\perp)>0$, the compression
$T\mapsto\omega(q^\perp Tq^\perp)/\omega(q^\perp)$ is an $N$-central state on
$\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)$.  On $N_+$ it is dominated by
$\omega(q^\perp)^{-1}\tau$, hence is normal; since $N$ is a factor its
restriction is $\tau$, contradicting relative proper proximality.  Thus
$\omega(q_i^\perp)=0$ for every $i$.

Extend $\omega$ to a state $\bar\omega$ on the ambient von Neumann algebra
$\cA_N$.  In the GNS representation of $\bar\omega$, each $q_i$ fixes the
cyclic vector.  Hence $R=(q_1q_2\cdots q_m)^*(q_1q_2\cdots q_m)$ satisfies
$\omega(R)=1$.  From $0\le R\le q_0$ we get $\omega(q_0)=1$.  DKEP's proof of
Lemma 8.5 supplies the natural $N$- and $JNJ$-bimodular u.c.p. map
\[
        \Theta:\mathbb B(L^2N)\longrightarrow q_0\cA_Nq_0,\qquad \Theta(1)=q_0,
\]
obtained from the canonical normal-bidual map.  Then $\omega\circ\Theta$ is an
$N$-central state on $\mathbb B(L^2N)$ extending $\tau$, a Connes hypertrace.
This contradicts non-amenability of the finite factor $N$.
\end{proof}

\section*{A relative Bass--Serre paradox}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:BS}
Let $N=P*_{B}Q$ with trace-preserving expectations and
$Q=B\bbarotimes A$.  Suppose that $A$ contains a trace-zero unitary $a$, and
that $P$ contains unitaries $w_1,w_2$ satisfying
\[
        E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
Then $N$ is properly proximal relative to $X_B$.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Use the reduced-word decomposition of $L^2(N)$ over $B$ and let $p$ and $q$ be
the projections onto the sums of reduced words whose first letter lies in
$L^2(P)\ominus L^2(B)$ and $L^2(Q)\ominus L^2(B)$, respectively.  Then
$1=e_B+p+q$.  If $R_x=Jx^*J$, direct inspection of reduced words gives, for
$x\in P\ominus B$,
\[
 [p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [q,R_x]=0,
\]
and the analogous formulas with $p,P$ and $q,Q$ interchanged for
$x\in Q\ominus B$.  Together with the trivial commutation for $x\in B$, DKEP
Lemma 6.1 gives $p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)$.

Let $\varphi$ be an $N$-central state on $S_{X_B}(N)$ normal on $N$.  Left
multiplication by $w_i$ sends the $Q$-initial subspace into the $P$-initial
subspace, and the two images are orthogonal because $E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0$; hence
$w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p$.  Also $a\in Q\ominus B$, so $apa^*\le q$.
Centrality gives $2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q)$, so
$\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0$.

Finally $h=w_1a$ is a $B$-Haar unitary: for $n\ne0$, $h^n$ is a non-trivial
reduced alternating word, so $E_B(h^n)=0$.  Thus the projections
$h^ne_Bh^{-n}$ are pairwise orthogonal and have the same $\varphi$-value,
forcing $\varphi(e_B)=0$.  This contradicts $1=e_B+p+q$.  By
\cite[Theorem 6.2]{DKEP}, $N$ is properly proximal relative to $X_B$.
\end{proof}

\section*{Application to graph products}

Fix $v\in V$ and put $L_v=\Lk_\Gamma(v)$.  The graph product decomposes as
\[
        M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}
        \bigl(M_{L_v}\bbarotimes M_v\bigr).                    \tag{5}
\]
Let $C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim_\Gamma v\}$.  Since $\Gamma^c$ is connected,
$C(v)\ne\emptyset$.  If $C(v)$ contains distinct $r,t$, choose trace-zero
unitaries $u_r\in M_r$, $u_t\in M_t$ and set $w_1=u_r$, $w_2=u_t$.  If
$C(v)=\{r\}$, connectedness of $\Gamma^c$ and $|V|\ge3$ give
$s\in L_v$ with $r\not\sim_\Gamma s$; choose trace-zero unitaries
$u_r\in M_r$, $u_s\in M_s$ and set $w_1=u_r$, $w_2=u_su_r$.  In both cases
reduced-word considerations give
\[
        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_i)=0,\qquad E_{M_{L_v}}(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
The given trace-zero unitary in $M_v$ supplies the unitary $a$ in the second
free-product leg of (5).  Proposition \ref{prop:BS} therefore shows that $M$
is properly proximal relative to $X_{M_{L_v}}$ for every $v\in V$.

Enumerate $V=\{v_1,\ldots,v_m\}$.  Since no vertex belongs to its own link,
$\cap_i L_{v_i}=\varnothing$.  Lemma \ref{lem:intersection} gives
\[
 q_{L_{v_m}}\cdots q_{L_{v_1}}q_{L_{v_2}}\cdots q_{L_{v_m}}
 \in q_\varnothing\cA_Mq_\varnothing .
\]
Here $M_\varnothing=\C$, and $X_{M_\varnothing}$ is the compact boundary piece,
because $e_\C$ is rank one and its left and right $M$-translates generate the
finite-rank operators.

It remains to verify the hypotheses of Lemma \ref{lem:finite}.  The algebra
$M$ is separable.  By \cite[Main Theorem 0.5]{CDHJKN}, factoriality can fail
only through a universal vertex, or through an exceptional non-edge pair
adjacent to every other vertex with both vertex algebras two-dimensional.
These would give respectively an isolated vertex or an isolated edge in
$\Gamma^c$, impossible because $\Gamma^c$ is connected and has at least three
vertices.  Hence $M$ is a factor.  Also $M$ is non-amenable: CDHJKN's
amenability characterization \cite[Proposition 6.3]{CDHJKN} would otherwise
force every non-edge of $\Gamma$ to be such an isolated exceptional pair,
again impossible for connected $\Gamma^c$ with at least three vertices.  Lemma
\ref{lem:finite} applies, and $M$ is properly proximal.
```

```latex
\section*{Problem interpretation}

The graph is finite, simple, and has at least three vertices.  The intended
``irreducible'' hypothesis is join-irreducibility: there is no nonempty proper
partition $V=S\sqcup T$ such that every vertex of $S$ is adjacent to every
vertex of $T$.  Equivalently the complement graph $\Gamma^c$ is connected.
All vertex traces are faithful normal traces.  A trace-zero unitary in each
$M_v$ implies each vertex algebra is nontrivial and provides centered unitaries
for reduced-word and amalgamated-free-product arguments.

For $U\subset V$, $M_U$ denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and
$E_U:M\to M_U$ the canonical expectation.  If $B\subset M$, $X_B$ is DKEP's
boundary piece generated by the Jones projection $e_B$.

\section*{DKEP normal-bidual notation}

Let
\[
  \mathcal A_N=(\mathbb B(L^2N)^\sharp_J)^*
\]
be DKEP's normal bidual.  For a boundary piece $X$, let $q_X$ be the support of
the normal corner $(\mathbb K_X(N)^\sharp_J)^*$ inside $\mathcal A_N$.  The
projection $q_X$ commutes with $N$ and with $JNJ$, and
\[
\widetilde S_X(N)=
\{T\in\mathcal A_N:[T,a]\in q_X\mathcal A_Nq_X
\text{ for every }a\in JNJ\}.
\]
For $X=\mathcal K(L^2N)$ write $q_0$ and $\widetilde S(N)$.

DKEP Lemma 8.5 applies to the trivial group action when $N$ is a separable
finite factor: $N$ is properly proximal relative to $X$ iff there is no
$N$-central state on $\widetilde S_X(N)$ whose restriction to $N$ is the trace.

Important caution: a state on a von Neumann algebra which is $1$ on two
noncommuting projections need not be $1$ on their lattice meet, because the GNS
representation of a non-normal state need not preserve meets.  Thus the naive
criterion ``relative to finitely many $X_i$ plus
$\bigwedge_iq_{X_i}=q_0$'' is not justified unless commutation/normality is
available.  The replacement is to use an actual positive palindromic product of
the $q_i$'s which lies in the compact corner.

\section*{Palindromic finite-boundary criterion}

Let $N$ be a separable nonamenable finite factor and $X_1,\dots,X_m$ boundary
pieces.  Put $q_i=q_{X_i}$.  Suppose
\[
  R=q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0.
\]
Then relative proper proximality with respect to every $X_i$ implies ordinary
proper proximality.

Proof details:
if $\omega$ is an $N$-central state on $\widetilde S(N)$ with $\omega|_N=\tau$,
then for each $i$,
\[
  q_i^\perp\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)q_i^\perp\subset \widetilde S(N)
\]
because $q_i$ commutes with $JNJ$ and $[T,JNJ]\subset q_i\mathcal A_Nq_i$.
If $\omega(q_i^\perp)>0$, compression by $q_i^\perp$ gives an $N$-central state
on $\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)$ normal on $N$ (normality is by domination by a scalar
multiple of $\tau$ on $N_+$), contradiction.  Hence the GNS cyclic vector for
$\omega$ is fixed by each $q_i$ and therefore by $R$.  Since $R$ is a positive
contraction in $q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0$, $1=\omega(R)\le \omega(q_0)$, so
$\omega(q_0)=1$.  Restricting $\omega$ to $q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\cong
\mathbb B(L^2N)$ gives a Connes hypertrace, contradicting nonamenability.

\section*{Graph-product boundary corner intersections}

For graph products write $q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}$.  The needed replacement for a meet
formula is
\[
  q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]
This implies by induction that the palindromic product associated with
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ lies in the corner for $\cap_iU_i$.

Reasoning.  Let $\mathcal H_U=L^2(M)\otimes_{M_U}L^2(M)$ with cyclic vector
$\zeta_U=1\otimes 1$.  The coefficient of $\zeta_U$ is $e_{M_U}$ and translates
of this vector generate DKEP's $X_{M_U}$.  DKEP's formula
$T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi$ shows that products of a $W$-coefficient, a
$U$-coefficient, and a $W$-coefficient are coefficients of
\[
  \mathcal H_W\otimes_M \mathcal H_U\otimes_M \mathcal H_W
\]
(up to hereditary closure; the cyclic coefficient is
$e_{M_W}e_{M_U}e_{M_W}$).  Thus the normal corner on the left is contained in
the coefficient support of this triple fusion.  CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and
Proposition 5.5 decompose this triple fusion as a direct sum of
$L^2(M)\otimes_{M_Z}L^2(M)$ with $Z\subset U\cap W$.  Since $X_{M_Z}\subset
X_{M_{U\cap W}}$, the whole coefficient boundary is contained in
$X_{M_{U\cap W}}$.

This is intentionally weaker than the unsupported identity
$q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}$ and is tailored to non-normal states.

\section*{Relative Bass--Serre paradox}

For $N=P*_BQ$, $Q=B\bar\otimes A$, with $A$ containing a trace-zero unitary $a$
and with unitaries $w_1,w_2\in P$ satisfying
\[
  E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0,
\]
one proves $N$ properly proximal relative to $X_B$.

Let $p$ and $q$ be the projections onto the free-product Fock subspaces whose
first letter is in $L^2(P)\ominus L^2B$ and $L^2(Q)\ominus L^2B$, respectively.
Then $1=e_B+p+q$.

Commutator formulas with right multiplication $R_x=Jx^*J$:
for $x\in P\ominus B$,
\[
  [p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [q,R_x]=0,
\]
and for $x\in Q\ominus B$,
\[
  [q,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [p,R_x]=0.
\]
Elements of $B$ commute with $p,q$.  These formulas are checked directly on
reduced words; only the $B$-summand and one-letter words from the same free
component can change first-letter type.  Since right translates of $e_B$ are in
the boundary ideal, DKEP Lemma 6.1 gives $p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)$.

If $\varphi$ is an $N$-central state on $S_{X_B}(N)$ normal on $N$, then
\[
  w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q.
\]
The first inequality uses centeredness of $w_i$ and orthogonality from
$E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0$; the second uses $a\in Q\ominus B$.  Centrality gives
$2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q)$, so $\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0$.
The unitary $h=w_1a$ is $B$-Haar: the subspaces $h^nL^2B$ are pairwise
orthogonal.  Hence centrality forces $\varphi(e_B)=0$, contradicting
$1=e_B+p+q$.

\section*{Graph-product application}

For each vertex $v$,
\[
  M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{\Lk(v)}}(M_{\Lk(v)}\bar\otimes M_v).
\]
Let $C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim v\}$.  Complement connectedness gives
$C(v)\ne\emptyset$.  If $C(v)$ has two vertices $r,t$, use trace-zero unitaries
$u_r,u_t$ and set $w_1=u_r,w_2=u_t$.  If $C(v)=\{r\}$, complement
connectedness and $|V|\ge3$ give $s\in\Lk(v)$ with $r\not\sim s$; set
$w_1=u_r,w_2=u_su_r$.  In both cases expectations of $w_i$ and $w_1^*w_2$ onto
$M_{\Lk(v)}$ vanish.  The vertex unitary in $M_v$ gives $a$.  Therefore $M$ is
properly proximal relative to every $X_{M_{\Lk(v)}}$.

The intersection of all links is empty.  The palindromic product of the link
boundary supports is therefore in the compact corner because
$M_\emptyset=\mathbb C$ and $X_{M_\emptyset}$ is the compact boundary piece.

CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 gives factoriality.  In a connected complement graph
with at least three vertices, there is no universal vertex and no isolated
exceptional nonedge component.  CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.1 gives nonamenability:
if $M$ were amenable, applying the relative amenability criterion with
$V_1=V,V_2=\emptyset$ would force every complement edge to be an isolated
exceptional edge, impossible in a connected complement graph with at least
three vertices.



\section*{Normal support calculus for correspondence boundaries}

For a correspondence $\mathcal H$, write $X_{\mathcal H}$ for DKEP's
coefficient boundary generated by $T_\xi^*T_\eta$ for bounded vectors.
Let $q_{\mathcal H}$ be the support in the normal bidual of
$\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}(M)$.  A useful way to view this support is via the
normal left ideal $\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}$ generated by the coefficient
operators $T_\xi$: the right support of $\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}$ is
$q_{\mathcal H}$, and $q_{\mathcal H}\mathcal A q_{\mathcal H}$ is the weak-*
closed hereditary corner generated by the coefficients.

For correspondences $\mathcal H,\mathcal K$ over $M$, DKEP's formula
$T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi$ gives the normal support-sandwich inclusion
\[
 q_{\mathcal H}(q_{\mathcal K}\mathcal A q_{\mathcal K})q_{\mathcal H}
 \subset
 q_{\mathcal H\otimes_M\mathcal K}\mathcal A
 q_{\mathcal H\otimes_M\mathcal K}.
\]
Proof template: first verify on generators
\[
T_\xi^*(T_\eta^*T_{\eta'})T_{\xi'}
 =T_{\xi\otimes\eta}^*T_{\xi'\otimes\eta'}.
\]
Then use separate weak-* continuity and approximate the right support
$q_{\mathcal H}$ by an approximate unit for
$\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}^*\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}$.  This avoids assuming
commutation of supports or identifying their lattice meet.

Direct sums: the coefficient boundary of $\bigoplus_i\mathcal H_i$ is supported
by the join of the supports of the $X_{\mathcal H_i}$.  Diagonal coefficients
are immediate; off-diagonal coefficients are controlled by the usual
$2\times2$ positivity/Douglas factorization argument.  Hence if every summand's
boundary is contained in $X_Y$, then the whole direct-sum boundary is contained
in $X_Y$.

For graph products, with $\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M$, CDHJKN fusion
gives
\[
  \mathcal H_W\otimes_M\mathcal H_U
  \cong \bigoplus_{Z\subset U\cap W}
       (L^2M\otimes_{M_Z}L^2M)^{\oplus k_Z}.
\]
Since $Z\subset I$ implies $e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_I}$ and $X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_I}$,
the support of the fusion is dominated by $q_I$.  Combining with the
support-sandwich inclusion yields
\[
  q_W(q_U\mathcal A q_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A q_{U\cap W}.
\]

\section*{Compact-corner hypertrace step}

In the finite-boundary criterion, do not identify
$q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0$ with $\mathbb B(L^2N)$.  DKEP's proof of Lemma 8.5 gives
the needed replacement: a natural $N$- and $JNJ$-bimodular u.c.p. map
\[
  \Theta:\mathbb B(L^2N)\to q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0
\]
sending $1$ to $q_0$ and $x\in N$ to $q_0xq_0$.  If a central state
$\omega$ on $\widetilde S(N)$ satisfies $\omega(q_0)=1$, then
$\omega\circ\Theta$ is an $N$-central state on $\mathbb B(L^2N)$ extending the
trace.  This is a Connes hypertrace, forcing amenability of the finite factor.



\section*{Round 6 support-calculus refinement}

The critic objected that the previous support-sandwich argument multiplied
concrete operators after embedding them in the DKEP normal bidual, where the
canonical embedding is only u.c.p. and not multiplicative.  The safer route is:

Let \(A=B(L^2N)\) and \(p=p_{\rm nor}\in A^{**}\) be the DKEP projection for
simultaneous normality over \(N\) and \(JNJ\).  For a DKEP boundary piece \(X\),
write \(e_X\in A^{**}\) for the ordinary support projection of
\(\mathbb K_X(N)\).  DKEP Proposition 2.10 implies \(e_Xp=pe_X\), and the
support projection in \(\mathcal A_N=pA^{**}p\) is \(q_X=pe_X\).  Thus one can
do support computations in the ordinary enveloping von Neumann algebra
\(A^{**}\), where the canonical embedding is multiplicative, and only then cut
by \(p\).

Concrete lemma: if \(B,C,D\subset A\) are hereditary \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodule
\(C^*\)-subalgebras and \(CBC\subset D\), then
\[
e_C(e_BA^{**}e_B)e_C\subset e_DA^{**}e_D.
\]
Proof: approximate an element of \(e_BA^{**}e_B\) weak-* by a bounded net from
\(B\), sandwich by an approximate unit of \(C\), and pass to weak-* limits.
Cutting by \(p\), using \(e_\bullet p=pe_\bullet\), gives
\[
q_C(q_B\mathcal A_Nq_B)q_C\subset q_D\mathcal A_Nq_D.
\]
This addresses the nonmultiplicativity warning: no claim is made that
\(\iota_{\rm nor}(xy)=\iota_{\rm nor}(x)\iota_{\rm nor}(y)\).

For coefficient boundary pieces, the relevant product is not the previously
claimed double fusion but a triple/coefficient product.  If \(\mathcal H\) and
\(\mathcal K\) are \(N\)-\(N\) correspondences, then for bounded vectors
\[
(T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})(T_{\xi_3}^*T_{\xi_4})
 =
T_{\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1}^*
T_{\xi_4\otimes\bar\xi_3\otimes\eta_2},
\]
using \(T_{\alpha\otimes\beta}=T_\beta T_\alpha\) and
\(T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*\).  Hence the algebraic coefficient product
\(X_{\mathcal H}X_{\mathcal K}X_{\mathcal H}\) is contained in the coefficient
boundary of
\(\mathcal H\otimes_N\overline{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K\).
DKEP Proposition 5.9/Theorem 5.10 and separate continuity of the relative
compact topologies are the intended justification for passing from algebraic
coefficient spans to the \(\mathbb K_X\)'s.

For graph products,
\(\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\) is self-contragredient.  Therefore
\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W
\subset
q_{\mathcal H_W\otimes\mathcal H_W\otimes\mathcal H_U}\mathcal A_M
q_{\mathcal H_W\otimes\mathcal H_W\otimes\mathcal H_U}.
\]
Applying the CDHJKN fusion rule twice decomposes this triple fusion into
summands \(\mathcal H_Z=L^2M\otimes_{M_Z}L^2M\) with \(Z\subset U\cap W\), so
the support is dominated by \(q_{U\cap W}\).  This gives the desired
palindromic-product inclusion without using non-normal lattice meets.


\section*{Refined normal-left-ideal support mechanism}

For an \(N\)-\(N\) correspondence \(\mathcal H\), DKEP's coefficient boundary
\(X_{\mathcal H}\) is generated hereditarily by the operators
\(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), \(\xi,\eta\) bounded.  The most economical support object is
not the concrete \(C^*\)-algebra product
\(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal K}}
\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\), but the weak-* closed normal left ideal
\(\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\) generated by the coefficient maps \(T_\xi\).  Its
right support in \(\mathcal A_N=(B(L^2N)^\sharp_J)^*\) is
\(q_{\mathcal H}\).  This follows because \(X_{\mathcal H}\) is the hereditary
algebra generated by \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), and DKEP Proposition 5.9 identifies the
corresponding relative compact operators with the operators whose positive
squares lie in the relevant \(\mathbb K_X\)-corners.

Composition of rectangular normal bimodules is separately weak-* continuous in
the DKEP normal-bidual setting and agrees on bounded vectors with the concrete
Connes-fusion formula
\[
  T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi.
\]
Hence
\[
  \mathcal L_{\mathcal K}\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}
  \subset \mathcal L_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\]
Since \(q_{\mathcal K}q_{\mathcal H}\in
\mathcal L_{\mathcal K}\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\), for any
\(x=q_{\mathcal K}a q_{\mathcal K}\) one obtains
\[
 q_{\mathcal H}xq_{\mathcal H}
 =(q_{\mathcal K}q_{\mathcal H})^*a(q_{\mathcal K}q_{\mathcal H})
 \in q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}\mathcal A_N
    q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\]
This is the support-level sandwich needed for graph products and avoids the
unproved closed-\(\mathbb K_X\) product inclusion.

For an expected inclusion \(B\subset N\), the induced correspondence
\(\mathcal H_B=L^2N\otimes_B L^2N\) has coefficient boundary \(X_B\).  The
cyclic vector \(\zeta=\hat1\otimes_B\hat1\) has coefficient operator \(e_B\),
and \(N\)-\(N\) translates of \(\zeta\) produce exactly the left/right
translates of \(e_B\) appearing in DKEP's definition of \(X_B\).

Direct sums of correspondences: if each summand \(\mathcal H_j\) has support
below a projection \(q\), then \(\oplus_j\mathcal H_j\) also has support below
\(q\).  Off-diagonal coefficients are controlled by the standard hereditary
corner fact \(a^*a,b^*b\in D\Rightarrow a^*b\in D\), proved from \(2\times2\)
positivity/Douglas factorization.  Infinite direct sums follow by truncating
bounded vectors.

\section*{Operator-system point in the palindromic criterion}

DKEP's \(\widetilde S_X(N)\) is an operator system, not generally a
\(C^*\)-algebra.  When a state \(\omega\) on \(\widetilde S(N)\) satisfies
\(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\) for several support projections \(q_i\), extend
\(\omega\) to a state \(\bar\omega\) on the ambient von Neumann algebra
\(\mathcal A_N\).  In the GNS representation of \(\bar\omega\), each
\(q_i\) fixes the cyclic vector; hence the palindromic product
\((q_1\cdots q_m)^*(q_1\cdots q_m)\) has \(\omega\)-value one.  This justifies
the GNS step without pretending that \(\widetilde S(N)\) itself is a
\(C^*\)-algebra.

Compression normality: if \(q=q_X\) and \(\omega(q^\perp)>0\), the compressed
functional \(T\mapsto \omega(q^\perp Tq^\perp)/\omega(q^\perp)\) is normal on
\(N\) because \(q\) commutes with \(N\) and, on \(N_+\), it is dominated by
\(\omega(q^\perp)^{-1}\tau\).
```

## Detailed audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

This section is acceptable. The original problem’s irreducibility condition is somewhat imprecise, and the answer explicitly adopts the standard graph-product interpretation: \(\Gamma\) is not a nontrivial join, equivalently \(\Gamma^c\) is connected. This is faithful to the intended graph-product usage.

The use of DKEP Lemma 8.5 for the normal-bidual criterion is appropriate once \(M\) is later shown to be a separable finite factor. DKEP’s Lemma 8.5 indeed gives the normal-bidual criterion for relative proper proximality under the relevant ergodicity/factor hypothesis. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### 2. Boundary support calculus

This is still the main problematic part.

#### 2.1 The support paragraph is mostly sourced, but does not solve the multiplication problem

The paragraph about \(p_{\rm nor}\), ordinary support projections, and nonunital inclusions is closer to DKEP than before. DKEP does describe \(p_{\rm nor}\), the identification \((A^\sharp)^*\cong p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\), and the inclusion for a hereditary subalgebra \(B\subset A\) using the support of \(B^{**}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

But DKEP also explicitly warns that the canonical map
\[
A\to (A^\sharp)^*
\]
is **not generally a \(*\)-homomorphism**. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) This warning is directly relevant to the new proof, because the proof later multiplies images of coefficient maps in \(\mathcal A_N\) and assumes those products behave like concrete operator composition.

#### 2.2 The “normal left ideal” \(\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\) is not adequately defined

The proof introduces a weak-* closed normal left ideal \(\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\subset \mathcal A_N\) “generated by the coefficient maps \(T_\xi\).” But DKEP defines \(T_\xi\) first as concrete bounded operators \(T_\xi\in B(L^2M,L^2N)\), and the formula
\[
T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi
\]
is a formula for concrete operator composition. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The revised proof does not rigorously explain:

- how the concrete \(T_\xi\)’s are embedded into the normal bidual \(\mathcal A_N\);
- why their products in \(\mathcal A_N\) agree with concrete products;
- why polar decomposition in \(\mathcal A_N\) applies to these \(T_\xi\)’s;
- why the right support of this newly defined ideal is exactly the DKEP support projection \(q_{\mathcal H}\).

These are not minor details. They are exactly where DKEP’s nonmultiplicativity warning becomes relevant.

#### 2.3 The key inclusion \(\mathcal L_{\mathcal K}\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\subset \mathcal L_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}\) is unproved

The proof asserts
\[
\mathcal L_{\mathcal K}\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}
\subset
\mathcal L_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\tag{2}
\]

The generator-level identity \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\) only proves something for products of two concrete coefficient maps. It does **not** prove the inclusion for products of arbitrary elements of the weak-* closed left ideals. An element of \(\mathcal L_{\mathcal K}\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\) has products of the form
\[
(aT_\eta)(bT_\xi),
\]
with arbitrary \(a,b\in\mathcal A_N\). The middle factor \(b\) is not handled by the concrete fusion formula.

The cited DKEP Corollary 5.2 and Proposition 5.9 do not state this normal-bidual left-ideal multiplication theorem. Corollary 5.2 concerns separate continuity in the \(X\)-\(Y\) topology for a different object, while Proposition 5.9 characterizes relative compactness of concrete rectangular operators. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Thus equation (2) remains an essential unproved lemma.

#### 2.4 The proof of Lemma `coefficient support sandwich` is not valid as written

The proof says:

\[
q_{\mathcal K}q_{\mathcal H}\in \mathcal L_{\mathcal K}\mathcal L_{\mathcal H},
\]
hence by (2)
\[
q_{\mathcal K}q_{\mathcal H}\in \mathcal A_Nq_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\]

But this depends entirely on the unproved inclusion (2). Moreover, if \(\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\) is a weak-* closed left ideal, then \(\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}=\mathcal A_Nq_{\mathcal H}\), so \(\mathcal L_{\mathcal K}\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\) is much larger than the span of products of concrete coefficient maps. The generator fusion formula does not control this whole product.

This is a fatal gap. The sandwich lemma is the bridge from relative boundary supports to graph-product fusion. Without it, the proof of the intersection lemma does not go through.

#### 2.5 The twofold fusion conclusion is especially suspect

The previous draft used a triple-fusion/coefficient-product heuristic. The revision now claims the support sandwich is controlled by the twofold fusion
\[
\mathcal H_W\otimes_M\mathcal H_U.
\]

But products of coefficient corners naturally look like
\[
(T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})(T_{\xi_3}^*T_{\xi_4}),
\]
which are controlled by a triple correspondence, not immediately by a twofold one. The new left-ideal argument is intended to bypass this, but because the left-ideal multiplication step is unproved, the twofold-fusion support bound is not justified.

#### 2.6 Direct sums are still only sketched

The proof says that if \(\mathcal H=\oplus_j\mathcal H_j\), then \(q_{\mathcal H}\) is dominated by the join of the \(q_{\mathcal H_j}\)’s, with off-diagonal coefficients controlled by \(2\times2\) positivity and infinite sums by truncation.

For finite sums this is plausible. For infinite direct sums, however, “truncating bounded vectors” is not automatically valid in the relevant operator norm/topology: Hilbert-norm convergence of vector truncations does not by itself imply norm convergence of the corresponding coefficient operators \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\). A proof is needed. Since CDHJKN’s decompositions may involve infinite multiplicities, this is another nontrivial missing support lemma.

DKEP does define coefficient boundary pieces for correspondences, but it does not provide in Example 5.11 the direct-sum support assertion in the form used here. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The reduced-word projections \(p,q\) and the commutator formulas with right multiplication are standard and believable. DKEP Lemma 6.1 indeed allows checking the commutator condition on an ultraweakly generating subalgebra. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This section is mostly correct, conditional on the unresolved boundary support machinery.

3. The product inclusion
   \[
   \mathcal L_{\mathcal K}\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}
   \subset
   \mathcal L_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}
   \]
   is not justified by the cited DKEP results and is much stronger than the concrete generator fusion formula.
4. The resulting coefficient support sandwich, and hence the graph-product boundary-corner intersection lemma, remain unproved.

Since this support calculus is essential to the proof, `answer.tex` is not yet a complete rigorous solution.
